speaker: interviewer
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me how Jasek Martko--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He left to Poland someplace because the account of the Ukrainians, they were-- they were doing very bad for the Polish people under Ukrainian. So he left. And then whatever he had left over in his farm, so I picked it up. And some of them he brought me. Now, later on-- later on, I was in this bunker. I was going for water with my father's cousin to a big farm where there was-- this man was the head of the forest. He was Polish. So we went--

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name. I don't remember his name. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me clarify something I made-- which bunker are we talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first. The first, where I found five more Jewish people from my city. We went for water. We got water in [POLISH]. In [POLISH]. They-- they are-- they lived in the-- in the forest, deep in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I took water-- this was 12 o'clock, midnight-- the lady, his wife, came out. And she said, Polish, who is taking water here? So when I hear Polish, then we answered that we are Jews. I'm here with my wife and a child, and we just passing by here. I don't want to tell her that we-- we have a bunker here. What was it like to see Jews in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest to see Jews was very, very-- sometimes you couldn't--you couldn't find nobody. But this was just an incident that those five friends of mine, from Krakowiec, they were in another bunker on the other side of Krakowiec. And the Ukrainians came, and they found them. They start shooting them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were 12. Seven got killed. Seven Jews got killed. And five men ran away. This is what they told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not what they told. That what I'm-- they told me, yeah. That they ran away. Now, when they ran away, they came to my forest where I was. And it was three days rain that time, and they were really wet and tired. And they didn't have where to stay. And I was going for water with my cousin. We were talking Jewish. 12, 1 o'clock. We were talking Jewish. And then they were in those forest just came in, and they heard us talking Jewish. So they say, Yidn! We are Jews too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I say, who are you? We are from Krakowiec. Oh. So I say, come on out. And when they came out, I know here is Ann Gottlieb. Here is-- all five. I knew them, all five.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told them that I have a bunker. They told me their story, that they ran away. They don't have where to stay no more. So I told them, I'm going to take you in my bunker. I have a big bunker. We are five people. So we going to have room for you too. We have a special place to make for-- only from wood and straw where to sleep. One line, the whole line. And that's what I took them in. And I gave them also the lady from that farm, the Polish lady, in the forest, that she can buy food for us, for money. Just to give them a $20 bill-- 20 gold dollar bills. So she used to buy all the food.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to share the space with so many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the space was not easy. But some of them were nice. Some of them were not appreciative. Some of them were not appreciative. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe--

speaker: interviewee
text: --but anyway, I know them before the war. One used to have a letter store. The other used to have a clothing store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Could you describe an event where it was hard to share the space?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you, when I didn't feel-- sometimes I didn't feel that they appreciate what I did for them, to take them in in my place. When they got food from somebody, one of the man, he had food from somebody else. And he didn't share with nobody. He just keeps it for himself, and he gave his brother. And when he gave a little [NON-ENGLISH], a dry piece of bread for my stepson when he was seven years old, he say, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. That mean eat, eat. Break your teeth. You see, it's not a nice word to say a little-- to a little kid, you know. So he was really a very rough character.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you--

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, you have to make the best. And that's way when I used to go stealing in private farms. I used to know where I'm going. I'm going stealing, I'm not going with there's man. I go where there's a widow. A rich widow, a lot of children, and they cannot go fight. When we came and I knocked on the door, in the window, I say, open up. I want food. There she opened up. And I say, we not came to fight, anything. We are hungry. We are Jews living far away from here. We don't have nothing to worry. Just let us take food, whatever we want. So we took it. And we took once a pig. We cut him up. Kill him, cut him up in pieces. Everybody had plastic where to take him. We had a butcher, actually, in those five guys. And that's the way I was going. I was the Fuhrer to show them where to go, not to be caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the sanitary conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sanitary is from rain. Sometimes we go to a lake at night. At night. But in the winter was rough. It was snow. Very bad. Big winters. Snow up to here. Three, four feet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we couldn't go out from the snow, all the steps to see. Now when people come sometime driving through, and she say-- see steps, who can go? Maybe it's only Jews. So we were afraid. So when it was a snowstorm, sometimes four or five days we couldn't go out. We were hungry. And in the beginning, we had potatoes. Potato peel. Once my wife made from potato peels, squashed it up, and make latkes. Potato pancakes. It was bitter. Terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you have to have something in the stomach. And snow-- water, we had for-- you know, from the snow we had water. We melted. We have water.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for your stepson?

speaker: interviewee
text: My stepson, he was very brave. Once he said, Daddy, you know what? You going out. You have no gun. You have nothing. You come out. What do you say? You don't have nothing to show. You have no gun. How about you do in a-- you-- you know everything. Why don't you take a piece of wood with a chisel, chisel out with a knife, and make it looking like a gun. Take a little leather from old shoes and sew it together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had with what to sew? Sew it together, and it'll look like a gun. When you'll go, you take out this and you show him, here. Give me everything. We don't do nothing. We're not going to shoot you. So he gave us idea. He gave us that idea. And it would-- would worked very good. One place-- one place we came in, we-- I took a steer. A big steer. I took him out. And a man came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, we hungry. We have to take it. And here. He saw the gun, he went in right away to the house. So we took the steer. The butcher, he was going with the steer, hold him. It was a young steer. Very powerful. Picking up his head, going up. And later, he got tired. I said, I'll take it on. Too bad I did it, because once the steer got scared. Something in the woods. And the steer got scared, and he picked himself up. I picked up myself with him. But then he stepped on my foot. And he stepped on my foot. My foot swell up like this for two months. For two months, I couldn't go out. My wife just to-- take me to the bunker, take me out. At that time, we were established already. I-- we prepared food. A lot of food we prepared. But one thing I was thinking, we lost potatoes from a sack. It got a hole, and we got scared because the head of the forest, the Polish man, he told us. He used to come in with his-- with his rifle, come to our bunker. And called us out, and we went out. He said, listen. You have to watch yourself. A lot of my men, Ukrainians, they told me that they found potatoes. Something is-- they want to call police to come check. I said, no. It's nothing. Don't worry. So here, watch yourself. From that time, I decided to build another bunker. If something happened what we don't know, somebody watches us slowly, and then they want to come with a group and catch us. Who can-- if we can run away, we have to have where to run. We can't just run from this section to diction. They'll go-- they'll shoot after you. I want to have another bunker where we can jump in and hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I build in a section farther away, I build another bunker. And then when you build up the other bunker, we went into that bunker. After a month later, start coming out water. A well came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: We needed water badly because we have to go far away for water. So this was very good. Well water. But then it start coming out more and more. We used to take the water out, throw out the water. Throw out. It come out in the hundreds of cans.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I say, it's no good. This is after three months. I say, we'll have to go. We came in the spring. I say, I'll go with [POLISH]. And we'll go and clean out the other bunker. Open 'em up. You know, fresh air go in. Clean it up. And we'll go back to the other bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what we did. After a couple days later, it was dry. And I took everything out from this bunker-- because the water keep coming so high up to our [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what it felt like to live in the forest? Your feelings about being there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived. And we used to say-- my wife used to say-- we saw once a cat in the forest. And my wife say, here the cat goes freely. She goes whatever she want. Nobody touches. But we are not free. We can show our face to nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the feeling, you know? That we are hunted and hunted and hunted. And we try to survive. Try to survive in the world, and-- and be menschen. Live like menschen. But it took a long time. Were you depressed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took a very long time. No. No. We not depressed. But just my wife was more depressed than me. She say, it's time to go. Let go. Let them take us. And forget about it. And all of that stuff. And my son was depressed, my little stepson was depressed. Once, we had soup. The soup got sour. And we couldn't eat. And he take it and he start vomiting. He couldn't eat. He couldn't eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he start running away. And then we see, where's-- where's Morris? Morris is not here. I start running after him, and I caught him before he went on-- where you running? Where are you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh, I'm going to a Gentile-- to a Gentile man or lady, and I'm going to tell them I'm a boy. I have no parents. Everybody got killed. My mother, my father got killed. Maybe you can give me something to eat. And later, I run away. I'll come back to the forest. But I caught him. So that was-- you know, depressing like another. Once we put up the food, a big pot of soup. And we put it up to cool it off. We cooked it in the bunker on that stove. And we put it to cool off. An hour later, we go to bring the soup down. There was a rat in the soup. Was a rat. Nobody could eat it. But one man, he ate it. One man ate the soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, what the heck? I'm hungry. I'm really very hungry. I can't take it. Later, he had cancer. But I don't know if he had cancer from that soup. Later in the United States, I know he died on cancer. So this kind of things, you know. Somebody can take. Somebody can't take. A rat. People eat rats, some-- you know-- Did you get sick?

speaker: interviewee
text: Get sick? No. In the woods, actually, I didn't get sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your son or your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But very skinny. My wife was like this. 60 pound. She weighed 60 pound. Very skinny. And my boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did you spend the day, your routine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the days, more often-- we lived from day to day, and that's the best thing to think, you know. Even today, it's all right, you know, with sickness, with this. You live from day to day. That time was different, you know, to survive from day to day. Then what's happened, what I want to tell you, about the second bunker. Now, I decided to go there. We packed everything up. We go to the other bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I figure we'll have water. We'll go for water here. We don't have to go three miles to go for water. So-- and I came with [POLISH]. We came with-- to take water, with a can. The whole bunker sink.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything dropped. All this-- all the separations, all the supports from the water start going. Every time we were taking out the water. And we would-- it would have fall on us another couple of days. We would have been killed in that bunker, because everything fall down. And I had mud like a meter and a half mud on top, and then trees on top. It was very heavy. And when it start molding and molding, slowly, slowly, we would have been covered completely. Would have been no way to get out. But it was lucky one night before. That one night, it's luck. And that's the way-- and then we-- you only get water from there. So once we were going-- before that, we were going for water, somebody almost caught us. But there's a lot of-- a lot of things going with our-- but it's too much to tell, this story. And then I build a bunker also for a Gentile man, what I used to have a station there. When I went out for food I get from somebody else, brought it to him. And over there, they have a little hand mill where I make my flour, where I make my barley, and the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I wouldn't remember now. Oh, this man-- this man, I know just-- I had a deal with him before the war. Before I left the village, I had a deal with him. And I bought a calf from, and that's the way I know. He was a very nice man. He had a wife. And once I went-- when I was-- when I build the bunker by him, I went to him and I dig everything. But we couldn't go home for three days, me and the boy-- and my cousin's boy. We couldn't go because there was another group of Ukrainian, they call themselves Bandera. Now, Bandera, he was against Jews, against the Polacks. He was going with the Germans. So they were controlling the whole area. I want to go through with all the food, with everything, whatever I go, they are here with lights, with shooting. For three days. My wife was over there already alone that time. Just with my stepson. Because my father's-- my father's cousin, she say one day I'm going to Jaworow. I know a lady. She used to work for me in my bakery. And I'm going to her, and I'm going to get food. I say, listen. You're making a mistake. You cannot go. You don't know the area. You from another area. You wouldn't know. I beg her to go and to go. One day, somehow, she say, no, no. I have to go to see her. She-- she went, but she never came back. They caught her. Somebody caught her. I-- realize maybe she's Jewish or whatever. They brought her to the Gestapo. So from that time, that time-- this is before I had-- when I had already the-- the bunker. I had the other one.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I figured when they're going to take her, usually they used to beat to death, the gestapo. Where is your husband? Where is your children? Where is your family? You have to tell us where they hide. When they beat, sometimes you have to go and tell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid that she is going to tell them. But she was very, very brave. And they beat her for three days, and she didn't say nothing. By that time, I left this bunker and I went to the other bunker. I feel-- figured when she is going-- when she is going to tell them, she is going to show them one bunker, not the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. How many bunkers did--

speaker: interviewee
text: She never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many bunkers did you build? Because I'm getting confused which one is which.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I had the bunkers what I will the ghetto was other things. So cousins were building--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: --bunkers. But in the forest, I had two bunkers. And one I had for a Gentile man in another forest, by-- by the village, by [POLISH]. So I build three bunkers, I built.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe when you came out of the bunker what you saw? What I envision-- what I envision what it was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean? The bunker?

speaker: interviewer
text: No. When you came out, what did it look like around you? Could you describe the scenery?

speaker: interviewee
text: The scenery. This was all in the woods. We didn't go-- you know, only at night. But in the daytime, we couldn't go. We stayed only in the woods in the bunker. At night, what can you see? It's nothing to see. But sometimes we have to go through a road, a expressway, where we have to go all over to another place where I was going to steal. Once, we went-- the whole group. All the men. 5, 6-- 5, 6, 7. We were seven-- seven men.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we have to go to another place. And we had to cross. Soon, we cross that road, that expressway. Two cars are coming with the lights. Coming closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: We jumped in on the other side of the road. And it was a little threes there. We hide in those trees. And just when they came by us, the two cars stopped. Two limousines stop. This was gestapo. It was German gestapo. And they stop just in the front on the highway, and we are on the other side deeper in little-- little woods. We couldn't breathe, nothing. We thought that maybe they saw us. But they didn't saw. They just had a flat tired. But you know, it took them an hour till they finished there to put in the flat tire, till they stretch themselves, and all of that. And meanwhile, we were scared to death and sitting, not a move. We couldn't move. We didn't have nothing to shoot. We-- if you have, you know, a rifle or whatever, you could shoot them. There were three in one car, three gestapo in the other car. But anyway, to start something like this, it's already a search for somebody who kill gestapo. The best way we figured out not to kill nobody, not to have anything involved, you know, that somebody should look in exactly for us. So we were very careful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were saying the gestapo was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And they-- later they moved away and they gone. And we went our way. And then we got-- now, the other thing is when I went to the gentleman where I build the bunker, and I had to go with all the food, everything on our shoulders, to carry everything, me and the boy. And we were going, and we couldn't go through. And sometimes we were going with the food, and somebody was shooting after us. We had to throw away. I pulled over from him all the food what they had on his back, and I say, take it from me. And I grabbed him by his hand, and we had to ran away and leave all the food. You couldn't run away. They were shooting from both sides. So-- and it was like 12, 1 o'clock at midnight. So we had to run fast. So I throw the food away, and we came back without food. So we were hungry for a while too. We went back.

speaker: interviewee
text: The second time when we went for three days, you couldn't go through. So my wife see one day I'm not there. Second day. On the third day, she say, uh huh. Somebody killed us. That's it. So he say. [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. She says, now you have no-- no daddy and I have no husband. What's going to happen? Let's go.

speaker: interviewee
text: She left everything, and she start going looking for us. She never been in this area at all. She never know the names even of the villages. To ask somebody. One miracle. One miracle was that I told her-- I say, sometime whatever will happen, you never know, I tell you where I am, where that Gentile person lives. There was a very rich man living on the other side of this man where I am. He's got a big farm, big beautiful house with a red roof. A red roof, cement. Cement blocks. Red roof.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told her his name is Korovitski. She got it in her mind. She remembered that this is Korovitski. Now, I was on the other side. So I say, I'm just on the opposite of the road about 200 feet, 300 feet in his-- he has a little forest. I'm across the street from Korovitski. So there I am. And that's it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when she left the bunker, she start going. She didn't know where to go. She started early in the morning. The way she was, ripped dress, the boy had ripped-- and ripped shoes. And walk and walk and walk. People saw her. Nobody say nothing. She couldn't ask nobody. She didn't know for what to ask. So she was going from village to village, village to village, till she came out through the woods somehow, and saw boys running around. Boys running around on tricycles-- on roller skates, sorry. On roller skates.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she say to let me wait. Maybe those boys would know something. Later when she saw further on, she see a red roof. And she saw that red roof, and the boys came closer. She say, boys, can you tell me, does here live-- she didn't want to say Korovitski-- does here lives a priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is Korovitski lives here. She didn't want to let them know that she knows, you know-- she wanted to hear that word, Korovitski. They say, no. This is not the priest. This is Korovitski.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she know already that this is Korovitski. She went over there. She had to go through a lake, all the way up to here, because you couldn't go the other way. You have to go to a bridge far away, which she couldn't find it. And where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was sitting in the hiding by that Gentile man, you know, across the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did she find--

speaker: interviewee
text: So when she came and she was-- they couldn't just go-- and it's going a little bit a-- a hill. When they came out across the street over the lake-- over the lake, when she came out they couldn't walk. Morris couldn't walk at all. She was dragging him like on all four, dragging him. And he came up. And they later-- and-- and they came up on top, they saw a house. So they say, must be that's the only house. So she came in the house. She knocked on the door. And we were sitting on the opposite way in a shack, not in the bunker. We were sitting in a shack.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were figuring when to go, when to leave. Maybe it will start again. So-- and she came up and she knocked the door. An old lady, the babushka, a grandma, came out. And she asked, babushka, is there my husband with a boy? She said, sh, sh, sh, sh. Yes. He is here. So right away her son came out. [POLISH] was his name. I don't remember his second name. He came out and he says, come on, I'll take you. I'll take you. They couldn't go because on account of shooting from the Bandera. So-- so later he brought some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away, he gave my wife and Morris, gave them food. And then he start-- we wait a whole other day. We are-- yeah. This was getting dark. She was going there. Soon it get like 11 o'clock, 11:30. We picked ourself with all the food, with everything. And we're crying. Crying our heart out as-- how she found us, she never been in this area. And miles and miles through water, through lakes, and a lot of-- they were-- they were also building-- drilling there like of coals. Oh, such a terrible holes. After holes after holes. Falling in and getting up. And she couldn't already walk. So later, we went slowly, slowly till we came back to our bunker. And we sit there. And it still was months and months till the Russians came.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. Around July, '44. June or July, '44. The Russians came. We were three days yet in the bunker. We didn't know. We didn't hear any shooting. Because we thought the Russians will come, and we'll hear fights with the Germans. Meanwhile, the Russians came to Lemberg. From Lemberg, they just couldn't cut through. They don't want to lose a lot of lives. So they went around Lemberg, leave out the whole area. No fighting. And then grabbed all the Germans in this area and took them-- took them away. And they didn't have-- they just came with the tanks rolling into Jaworow. The opposite way. So I didn't know. We hear, we thought it's going on a fight. In the meanwhile, on the third day, we went to that Polish man. We went there. We ask him. He say, the Russians are here three days. He said, I thought that you know already. And then we went out. So we went out, all ripped the clothes and everything. So my wife with Morris, she took Morris by the hand. So she came to the road. And we were sitting-- we were sitting in between trees. They shouldn't think that we are partisans. They were also a lot of Ukrainian partisans were shooting the Russian. The Russians. So we-- she was afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she-- so soon they see her, they stopped. She picked up her hand in the middle of the road, and she hold-- a whole squadron of tanks. They all stopped. And they ask her, in Russian, [SPEAKING RUSSIAN]. What kind of people are you? She say, we are not partisans. We are Jews. Survivors. And I got my husband. And my husband saw this, and this, told them. Later, they took us all on the tanks, on top of the tanks. They'd drag us up, and they took us to Jaworow. Where you want to go, they ask? We say, to Jaworow. And that's how we came to Jaworow. Could you describe what you felt that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I felt a little easier. Much easier. Much easier till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I start doing a little work against the Ukrainians, for they were killing Jews. And my wife know a lot what she heard, saying those Ukrainians, they say, why don't you throw-- oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: One says to the other, why don't you throw in the-- in the well? One girl, a girl friend from my wife. Why don't you throw the girl? He grabbed her and he throw her. He drowned her. Throwed her in a well. So later when he came-- Excuse me. Who was thrown in the well?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Ukrainian. Ukrainians. Bandera-- those Bandera. They throw her in a well. But my wife overheard it in the-- in the house. And they passed by. Why don't you throw her in a well? They throw her in the well.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're taking a pause now, and going to the next tape.
